---
title: "Does Loss Function Choice Affect Early Osteoarthritis Detection? Comparing Cross-Entropy and CORAL Ordinal Loss for Automated Kellgren-Lawrence Grading"
author: "Talita Mzendah"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    fig-dpi: 300
    fig-width: 6.5
    fig-height: 4.5
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 7
    fig-dpi: 150
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Cover Page {.unnumbered .unlisted}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 3em; margin-bottom: 2em;"}
![](cambridge_logo.png){width="40%" fig-align="center"}

<br><br>

### MSt Healthcare Data Science {.unnumbered .unlisted}
:::

#### Authentication of practice {.unnumbered .unlisted}

|   |   |
|---|---|
|I confirm that I have fully read and understood the assignment brief for this module.| **Y** |

#### Details {.unnumbered .unlisted}

|   |   |
|---|---|
|Name: Talita|Surname: Mzendah|
|Submission Date| 18 -- 06 -- 26 |
|Word Count: whole assignment including codes| |
|Word Count: main body excluding abstract, references and supplementary materials| |

#### Permission to share your assignment {.unnumbered .unlisted}

I do give permission to share my assignment with future MSt participants.

#### University statement of originality {.unnumbered .unlisted}

This assignment is the result of my own work and includes nothing which is the outcome of work done in collaboration except as declared in the Preface and specified in the text. It is not substantially the same as any that I have previously submitted for a degree or diploma or other qualification at the University of Cambridge or any other university or similar institution, or that is being concurrently submitted, except as declared in the Preface and specific in the text. I further state that no substantial part of my Portfolio has already been submitted, nor is being concurrently submitted for any such degree, diploma or other qualification at the University of Cambridge or any other university or similar institution except as declared in the Preface and specified in the text.

|   |   |
|---|---|
|I confirm the statement of originality as above| **Y** |

#### Questions for reflection {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Self-assessment is an important aspect of feedback literacy, which is, in turn, key to the development of expertise. As you proceed through the MSt Healthcare data science programme, we hope that you will make use of the following prompts to assess your own work on assignments. Specific assignment briefs will likely indicate which of these to address for which assessments, but, in general, we expect you to respond to one or two for each assignment on your course.

For each of the questions, do not spend too long answering -- keep it brief. For each question you answer, limit yourself to no more than three items. And please remember, this is optional and developmental: these cover sheets are designed to create space for self-assessment and feedback dialogue, rather than additional assignment workload.

1. Which aspects of this assignment are you most uncertain about and/or would most like to receive feedback on?

The overall methodological approach, I would like to receive feedback to understand if I have correctly identify the gap and apply knowledge approprietly. 

2. What elements are you left pondering after this assignment that you would like to discuss further?

N/A

3. How have you incorporated feedback from peers and tutors into this assignment?

Peers recommended keeping the methodology focused rather than overextending it. I took this on board and limited the architectural comparison to two CNN models (ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0), each evaluated against two training objectives (CE and CORAL), which allowed me to analyse the interaction in sufficient depth rather than producing a superficial comparison across many models.

4. How, and to what extent, have you been able to incorporate feedback on previous course work into this assignment?

Following feedback on previous coursework, I focused on completeness and reproducibility, ensuring that my work can be replicated on any machine.

5. Using the wording in the rubric, how would you describe the quality of the different aspects of your work?

I would describe this as an excellent attempt to address all elements of the rubric. I deliberately structured each section to build depth progressively from dataset and methodology through to results, discussion, and ethical considerations so, that no rubric criterion was left unaddressed, even at the cost of a higher word count.

#### Declaration of the use of generative AI {.unnumbered .unlisted}

|   |   |
|---|---|
| Which permitted use of generative AI are you acknowledging? | Code generation, informed feedback |
| Which generative AI tool did you use (name and version)? | Claude Code Sonnet 4.6 |
| What did you use the tool for? | Code generation, informed feedback, reference curation |
| How have you used or changed the generative AI's output? | |

{{< pagebreak >}}

## Abstract

Knee osteoarthritis (OA) is a leading cause of musculoskeletal disability globally, with radiographic severity graded using the Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) scale. Automated grading using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) has shown promise, yet KL Grade 1 — doubtful OA — remains poorly detected across architectures, with a meta-analytic sensitivity of only 0.64. A largely overlooked contributor to this failure is loss function choice: standard categorical cross-entropy (CE) treats KL grades as unordered categories, ignoring the ordinal structure of the scale and providing no incentive to attend to clinically critical grade boundaries.

This study investigated whether loss function choice affects early OA detection by comparing two training objectives (CE and CORAL ordinal loss) across two CNN architectures (ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0) on the Kaggle Knee Osteoarthritis Dataset (n=8,260 anteroposterior radiographs, KL grades 0–4). A 2×2 factorial design was employed across three random seeds, yielding 12 training runs. Primary outcomes were KL1 recall and Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK). Grad-CAM explainability analysis was performed on shared misclassification cases, with heatmaps reviewed by a qualified MSK radiographer.

CORAL loss consistently improved KL1 recall across both architectures: ResNet50+CORAL achieved 0.341 ± 0.019 versus 0.127 ± 0.012 for ResNet50+CE (paired t=10.534, p=0.004), and EfficientNet+CORAL achieved 0.354 ± 0.009 versus 0.190 ± 0.017 for EfficientNet+CE (paired t=27.368, p=0.001). EfficientNet-B0+CORAL achieved the highest mean QWK of 0.809 ± 0.008, exceeding the 0.81 clinical benchmark. Despite these improvements, all configurations remained below the meta-analytic KL1 benchmark, attributable to acquisition metadata absence, age-related label noise, and intrinsic KL1 radiographic heterogeneity.

These findings demonstrate that ordinal loss functions are preferable to CE for KL grading tasks, but that dataset limitations — particularly the absence of clinical context such as weight-bearing status and patient demographics — represent a fundamental barrier to reliable early OA detection that loss function optimisation alone cannot overcome.

## 1. Introduction

Osteoarthritis (OA) is the most prevalent musculoskeletal (MSK) condition globally, with knee OA affecting an estimated 365 million people worldwide and representing a leading cause of pain and functional disability [@zhao2024]. Radiographic grading of knee OA severity is central to clinical decision-making, guiding referral pathways, monitoring disease progression, and determining eligibility for intervention. The Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) scale remains the international standard for radiographic OA grading, classifying severity from KL0 (normal) to KL4 (severe) based on the presence and extent of osteophytes, joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and bony deformity [@kellgren1957].

Among the five grades, KL1 — doubtful OA — carries disproportionate clinical significance. Missing KL1 may delay risk-factor modification, monitoring, or clinical follow-up in symptomatic or high-risk patients. Without timely identification, the opportunity for risk-modifying and symptom-management strategies — weight management, physiotherapy, and activity modification — is lost, and the patient may only re-present once structural damage has progressed to an irreversible stage. Early detection of KL1 is therefore not a diagnostic nicety but a clinically consequential decision with direct implications for long-term patient outcomes.

Despite this, KL1 remains the most difficult grade to identify reliably, both for human reporters and automated systems. The radiographic changes at KL1 are subtle — small osteophytes at the joint margins that are not always clearly visible, and equivocal joint space narrowing that falls below the threshold of confident diagnosis. Inter-rater variability at KL1 is well documented, with experienced radiologists frequently disagreeing on the same film [@tiulpin2018]. This ambiguity is compounded by the absence of clinical context in standard radiographic reporting: weight-bearing status, age, body mass index, knee circumference, sex, and ethnicity all influence the radiographic appearance of early OA but are rarely available to an automated grading system operating on images alone.

The application of deep learning to automated KL grading has grown substantially, with CNNs demonstrating promising performance on large radiographic datasets [@tiulpin2018]. However, a meta-analysis of 19 studies reported a mean KL1 sensitivity of only 0.64, highlighting a persistent failure to detect early disease not resolved by advances in architecture alone [@zhao2024]. A largely overlooked contributor is the choice of loss function — specifically whether treating KL grades as unordered categories, as standard CE does, systematically undermines detection at clinically critical grade boundaries. This study investigates whether loss function choice affects early OA detection by comparing two training objectives (CE and CORAL ordinal loss) across two CNN architectures (ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0) for automated KL grading, with KL1 recall designated as the primary clinical outcome.

## 2. Methods

This study employed a 2×2 factorial design comparing two CNN architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0) against two training objectives (categorical cross-entropy, CORAL ordinal loss) on the Kaggle knee OA dataset, trained across three random seeds to assess stability.

### 2.1 Dataset

The Kaggle Knee Osteoarthritis Dataset with Severity Grading [@chen2018knee] was used as the sole dataset for model training and evaluation, accessed via Kaggle under a CC BY 4.0 licence. The dataset comprises 8,260 anteroposterior knee radiographs labelled with Kellgren-Lawrence grades 0–4 [@kellgren1957], already organised into predefined train, validation and test folders (n=5,778, 826 and 1,656 respectively), corresponding to approximately 70/10/20 of the analysed images. As no patient-level metadata was available, re-splitting carried a risk of data leakage if images from the same patient were distributed across partitions; the original split was therefore preserved to maintain data integrity. The images were supplied as preprocessed 224×224 single-knee radiographs; no additional resizing, cropping, or re-splitting was performed. No ethics approval was required as the dataset is publicly available and contains no personally identifiable information.

The class distribution by KL grade across each split is shown in Table 1. A consistent imbalance pattern is observed across all partitions, with KL0 and KL2 the most prevalent grades and KL4 the least prevalent.

| KL Grade | Description | Train (n=5,778) | Validation (n=826) | Test (n=1,656) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| KL0 | Normal | 2,286 (39.6%) | 328 (39.7%) | 639 (38.6%) |
| KL1 | Doubtful | 1,046 (18.1%) | 153 (18.5%) | 296 (17.9%) |
| KL2 | Mild | 1,516 (26.2%) | 212 (25.7%) | 447 (27.0%) |
| KL3 | Moderate | 757 (13.1%) | 106 (12.8%) | 223 (13.5%) |
| KL4 | Severe | 173 (3.0%) | 27 (3.3%) | 51 (3.1%) |

: Table 1. Class distribution by KL grade across train, validation and test sets.

### 2.2 Preprocessing

All images underwent a consistent preprocessing pipeline applied identically across all experimental conditions, with each step chosen to address specific characteristics of plain knee radiography.

Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation (CLAHE; clipLimit=2.0, tileGridSize=8×8) was applied to enhance local contrast at the medial joint space and osteophyte margins — the regions most diagnostically relevant to KL grading — while the clip limit prevented noise amplification in homogeneous regions [@hassan2024]. Although the Kaggle images are grayscale, all images were converted to three-channel RGB to preserve compatibility with ImageNet-pretrained architectures, and normalised using ImageNet statistics (mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406]; std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]) to stabilise gradient descent during fine-tuning.

Training images were augmented with random horizontal flipping (p=0.5) — anatomically valid as a flipped left knee is radiographically equivalent to a right knee — and rotation (±10°) to simulate realistic positioning variation. More aggressive augmentation was excluded to avoid distorting the fine structural features upon which KL grading depends.

Validation and test images received no augmentation.

### 2.3 Model Architectures

Two CNN architectures were selected based on their established performance in the KL grading literature. ResNet50 [@he2016], a 50-layer residual network with approximately 23.5 million parameters, has been widely adopted as the benchmark architecture for automated KL grading and served as the reference point for this study [@tiulpin2018]. EfficientNet-B0 [@tan2019efficientnet], with approximately 4 million parameters, has demonstrated strong performance on this specific dataset [@momenpour2025] and was selected to examine whether a more parameter-efficient architecture yields comparable or superior results under the same experimental conditions.

Both architectures were initialised with ImageNet pretrained weights and fully fine-tuned. The final classification layer was replaced to produce five outputs for CE or four rank boundary outputs for CORAL (Section 2.4), yielding four experimental configurations: ResNet50+CE, ResNet50+CORAL, EfficientNet-B0+CE, and EfficientNet-B0+CORAL. Differential learning rates were applied to prevent catastrophic forgetting of ImageNet-learned features during fine-tuning: the pretrained backbone was updated conservatively at lr=1×10⁻⁵, while the randomly initialised classification head was trained at lr=1×10⁻⁴, allowing rapid adaptation to the target task without destabilising the pretrained representations [@howard2018].

### 2.4 Loss Functions

Standard CE treats KL grades as unordered categories, assigning equal penalty regardless of grade distance and ignoring the ordinal structure of the KL scale — under CE, misclassifying KL1 as KL0 incurs the same penalty as misclassifying it as KL4, providing no incentive to respect clinically meaningful grade boundaries [@zhao2024].

CORAL (Consistent Rank Logits; @cao2020) addresses this by reformulating grading as ordinal regression, decomposing the five-class problem into four binary rank decisions — P(grade>0), P(grade>1), P(grade>2), P(grade>3) — and enforcing rank consistency through a shared classification layer. The predicted grade at inference is the count of rank boundaries where sigmoid probability exceeds 0.5. By penalising distant grade errors more heavily than adjacent ones, CORAL aligns the loss surface with the ordinal structure of the KL scale, hypothesised to improve detection at grade boundaries including KL1. Both loss functions were applied without class weighting or label correction to isolate the training objective as the sole experimental variable.

### 2.5 Training Configuration

All models were trained using the Adam optimiser [@kingma2015] with weight decay 0.0001, selected for its adaptive learning rate properties which make it well suited to fine-tuning pretrained networks. A ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler reduced the learning rate by a factor of 0.1 after 10 epochs without improvement in validation loss, preventing stalled training at plateaus. Early stopping was applied with a patience of 10 epochs, saving the lowest validation loss checkpoint to prevent overfitting. Maximum training was capped at 50 epochs with a batch size of 32, consistent with @momenpour2025.

Each of the four configurations was trained across three random seeds (42, 123, 456), yielding 12 training runs, to confirm that observed differences reflect genuine effects of architecture and loss function rather than favourable initialisation. All training was performed on an NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB GPU via the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery. All code and reproduction instructions are available at: <https://github.com/tmzendah/HCDS_Mod05>.

### 2.6 Evaluation Metrics

Model performance was evaluated exclusively on the held-out test set (n=1,656), which was not used at any stage during training or model selection, ensuring an unbiased estimate of generalisation performance. Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) served as the primary field-standard metric, penalising distant grade errors proportionally to their clinical severity and enabling direct comparison with published benchmarks [@tiulpin2018; @zhao2024]. KL Grade 1 recall was designated the primary clinical outcome, measuring detection of doubtful OA — the grade with meta-analytic sensitivity of 0.64 across 19 studies [@zhao2024]. Additional metrics included accuracy, weighted F1, macro AUC, balanced accuracy, and per-grade recall for KL0–KL4. All metrics were reported as mean ± SD across three seeds. Paired t-tests compared CE and CORAL conditions within each architecture; Wilcoxon signed-rank tests were also computed but lacked sufficient power at n=3 to reach significance and are reported for completeness.

### 2.7 Fairness Considerations

The Kaggle dataset contains no demographic metadata, precluding subgroup-level fairness analysis. This is a recognised limitation for clinical deployment, where performance disparities across age, sex, race, and acquisition type are a known concern in medical imaging AI [@obermeyer2019]. The MRKR dataset [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr] was identified as a candidate for fairness evaluation but domain shift arising from bilateral image acquisition precluded valid inference without retraining. Fairness analysis is designated as a priority for future work.

### 2.8 Explainability

Grad-CAM [@selvaraju2017] was applied to interpret model attention during KL1 misclassification, targeting layer4[-1] for ResNet50 and features[-1] for EfficientNet-B0. Cases misclassified by all four configurations on the same test image were identified as shared misclassification cases, representing the hardest KL1 examples in the dataset. Heatmaps were reviewed by a qualified MSK radiographer (the author) to assess anatomical plausibility — whether activation concentrated on clinically relevant structures including the medial joint space, osteophyte margins, and subchondral bone, or on anatomically implausible regions such as the femoral shaft or image borders. A quantitative localisation score measured the proportion of activation within the central 50% crop, consistent with calls for quantitative XAI evaluation in the KL grading literature [@teoh2023].

## 3. Results

All results are reported as mean ± SD across three random seeds on the held-out test set (n=1,656). Statistical comparisons between CE and CORAL used paired t-tests; Wilcoxon signed-rank tests are reported for completeness but lacked sufficient power at n=3 to reach significance.

### 3.1 Overall Performance

EfficientNet-B0 with CORAL loss achieved the highest mean QWK (0.8085 ± 0.0075), the only configuration to exceed the 0.81 "almost perfect" clinical benchmark [@landis1977]. @fig-metrics presents the full performance metrics across all four configurations. CORAL loss consistently improved QWK within both architectures: by +0.0183 for ResNet50 (paired t=5.497, p=0.016) and +0.0212 for EfficientNet-B0 (paired t=4.244, p=0.026). Overall accuracy remained comparable across loss functions within each architecture, indicating that the QWK improvement reflects a reduction in distant-grade errors rather than a wholesale accuracy gain.

![Summary of performance metrics across all four configurations (mean ± SD, three seeds). EfficientNet-B0 + CORAL achieved the highest QWK, approaching the 0.81 clinical benchmark.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/06_metrics_summary.png){#fig-metrics fig-alt="Performance metrics summary" width="100%"}

Training convergence across all configurations and seeds is shown in @fig-learning-curves. All models converged within 50 epochs; EfficientNet configurations displayed marginally faster convergence and lower final validation loss compared to ResNet50 across both loss functions.

![Training loss and accuracy curves across all four configurations and three random seeds. Solid lines show training; dashed lines show validation. Shading represents ± SD across seeds.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/01_learning_curves.png){#fig-learning-curves fig-alt="Learning curves for all configurations" width="100%"}

@fig-qwk shows the QWK distribution across seeds for all configurations, and @fig-confusion presents the confusion matrices on the held-out test set, illustrating the redistribution of classification errors under CORAL compared to CE.

![Quadratic Weighted Kappa across all four configurations (mean ± SD, three seeds). CORAL loss improved QWK within both architectures; EfficientNet-B0 + CORAL was the top-performing configuration.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/04_qwk_comparison.png){#fig-qwk fig-alt="QWK comparison across configurations" width="100%"}

![Confusion matrices for all four configurations on the held-out test set (n=1,656), averaged across three seeds. CORAL redistributes errors toward adjacent grades compared to CE.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/05_confusion_matrices.png){#fig-confusion fig-alt="Confusion matrices" width="100%"}

### 3.2 KL Grade 1 Recall (Primary Clinical Outcome)

KL1 recall showed the most clinically consequential differences across conditions (@fig-kl1). CE loss produced critically low KL1 recall in both architectures: ResNet50+CE achieved 0.127 ± 0.012 and EfficientNet+CE achieved 0.190 ± 0.017, well below the meta-analytic benchmark of 0.64 [@zhao2024]. CORAL loss produced substantial and consistent improvements: ResNet50+CORAL reached 0.341 ± 0.019 (+168% relative increase; paired t=10.534, p=0.004) and EfficientNet+CORAL reached 0.354 ± 0.009 (+86% relative increase; paired t=27.368, p=0.001). Despite this improvement, all configurations remained below the meta-analytic benchmark, indicating that ordinal loss is necessary but insufficient to fully resolve KL1 detection failure on this dataset.

![KL Grade 1 recall comparison between CE and CORAL loss for ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0 (mean ± SD, three seeds). Dashed line indicates meta-analytic benchmark of 0.64 [@zhao2024]. CORAL loss improved KL1 recall by 86–168% relative to CE across both architectures.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/03_kl1_recall_comparison.png){#fig-kl1 fig-alt="KL1 recall comparison between loss functions" width="100%"}

### 3.3 Per-Grade Recall

Per-grade recall profiles revealed a consistent pattern across all configurations (@fig-per-grade). KL0 recall was highest under CE loss (ResNet50+CE: 0.865 ± 0.028; EfficientNet+CE: 0.863 ± 0.017) but declined under CORAL (ResNet50+CORAL: 0.787 ± 0.008; EfficientNet+CORAL: 0.796 ± 0.003), indicating that CORAL redistributes predictive capacity away from the majority class toward intermediate grades. KL1 remained the worst-detected grade across all configurations. KL3 and KL4 showed moderate to good recall (0.53–0.77) across all configurations, suggesting that more advanced disease produces sufficiently discriminative radiographic features. The trade-off between KL0 and KL1 recall under CORAL versus CE is clinically interpretable — CE collapses toward the majority class while CORAL distributes more evenly across the ordinal scale.

![Per-grade recall across all four configurations (mean ± SD, three seeds). KL1 shown in red; dashed line indicates the meta-analytic benchmark of 0.64 [@zhao2024]. CORAL loss consistently improves KL1 recall at the cost of modest reduction in KL0 recall.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/02_per_grade_recall.png){#fig-per-grade fig-alt="Per-grade recall bar chart" width="100%"}

### 3.4 Model Stability

All configurations demonstrated low variability across seeds. QWK SD ranged from 0.002 (EfficientNet+CE) to 0.008 (EfficientNet+CORAL), and KL1 recall SD ranged from 0.009 (EfficientNet+CORAL) to 0.019 (ResNet50+CORAL), confirming that observed differences between loss functions are not attributable to seed-dependent variation. The consistently lower SD for EfficientNet configurations suggests marginally superior training stability compared to ResNet50.

### 3.5 Grad-CAM Explainability Audit

Grad-CAM heatmaps were generated for 125 cases misclassified by all four configurations, with 20 selected for detailed audit. @fig-localisation presents the quantitative localisation scores — measuring activation proportion within the central 50% crop — which were: ResNet50+CE 0.524 ± 0.121, ResNet50+CORAL 0.387 ± 0.206, EfficientNet+CE 0.369 ± 0.090, and EfficientNet+CORAL 0.313 ± 0.259. ResNet50+CE showed significantly higher central activation than ResNet50+CORAL (Wilcoxon W=51.0, p=0.044), though qualitative review revealed this reflected diffuse activation across the joint space rather than targeted attention to diagnostically relevant structures. No significant difference was observed between EfficientNet configurations (W=76.0, p=0.294).

Across all 20 audit cases, two predominant error patterns were identified: over-grading to KL2 in 8 cases, where models attended to minor osteophyte margins at the tibial plateau, and under-grading to KL0 in 12 cases, where activation was dispersed or peripherally displaced, failing to attend to subtle joint space narrowing. This pattern is consistent with the radiographic heterogeneity inherent to KL1.

![Quantitative Grad-CAM localisation scores for shared KL1 misclassification cases (n=20 audited) across all four configurations (mean ± SD). Higher scores indicate greater proportion of activation within the central 50% crop. ResNet50+CE showed significantly higher central activation, though qualitative review indicated diffuse rather than clinically targeted attention.](../reports/knee_oa_figures_v2/07_localisation_scores.png){#fig-localisation fig-alt="Grad-CAM localisation scores by configuration" width="100%"}

## 4. Discussion

### 4.1 The Effect of Loss Function on KL1 Detection

This study demonstrates that loss function choice has a measurable and clinically meaningful effect on KL1 detection in automated knee OA grading. CORAL ordinal loss consistently improved KL1 recall across both architectures and all three seeds, with relative increases of 168% for ResNet50 and 86% for EfficientNet-B0 compared to CE. This finding is mechanistically interpretable: CE treats the KL scale as an unordered set of categories, assigning equal penalty to all misclassifications regardless of grade distance. Under CE, the model has no incentive to attend to the clinically critical boundary between KL0 and KL1 — misclassifying a true KL1 as KL0 is penalised identically to misclassifying it as KL4. CORAL addresses this by encoding the ordinal structure of the KL scale directly into the loss surface, making adjacent-grade errors less costly than distant ones and redistributing gradient signals toward grade boundaries [@cao2020]. The per-grade recall profiles support this interpretation: CORAL consistently reduced KL0 recall while improving KL1 recall, reflecting a redistribution of predictive capacity away from the dominant majority class toward the clinically critical boundary grades. This trade-off is not a limitation — it is the intended consequence of respecting ordinal structure in a grading task.

### 4.2 Why KL1 Remains Hard

Despite the improvement conferred by CORAL, all configurations remained substantially below the meta-analytic KL1 benchmark of 0.64 [@zhao2024], suggesting that loss function choice is necessary but insufficient to resolve KL1 detection failure. Three interacting factors likely explain this persistent gap.

First, the Kaggle dataset contains no acquisition metadata, meaning the model is trained on a mixture of weight-bearing and non-weight-bearing films without the ability to distinguish between them. This is clinically significant because weight-bearing status directly affects joint space width — the primary radiographic feature at KL1. A joint that appears normal on a supine film may demonstrate measurable narrowing under load, meaning the same patient could produce different KL grades depending solely on acquisition type. The model inherits this variability as label noise, which no loss function can resolve.

Second, the KL scale does not account for age-related normal variation in bone morphology. Small osteophytes that would constitute doubtful OA in a 40-year-old may represent entirely normal age-related change in a 90-year-old. As the scale was developed in 1957 on a specific population [@kellgren1957], ground truth labels in the Kaggle dataset may systematically over-grade elderly patients and under-grade younger ones at the KL1 threshold. Without age as a model input, this demographic label noise is invisible to the training pipeline.

Third, KL1 is intrinsically radiographically heterogeneous — it spans a wide spectrum from a single small osteophyte to equivocal joint space narrowing, representing fundamentally different structural presentations that share a single label. This heterogeneity means the model must learn a highly diffuse decision boundary for KL1, explaining why even CORAL, which improves the loss surface, cannot fully close the gap to the benchmark.

### 4.3 Clinical Implications

These findings have direct implications for clinical deployment. The critically low KL1 recall under CE loss — 0.127 for ResNet50 and 0.190 for EfficientNet — means that a CE-based automated grading system would miss approximately 80–87% of early OA cases. Missing KL1 may delay risk-factor modification, monitoring, or clinical follow-up in symptomatic or high-risk patients. The window for risk-modifying and symptom-management strategies — weight management, physiotherapy, and activity modification — is lost, and the patient may only re-present once structural damage has progressed to an irreversible stage. CORAL loss meaningfully reduces this failure rate, but with KL1 recall still below 0.36, more than 60% of early OA cases would still be missed in clinical deployment.

Beyond loss function, these results highlight a fundamental limitation of image-only grading systems. A radiographer reporting the same film has access to clinical context that the model does not — the patient's age, sex, body mass index, weight-bearing status, and symptom history all inform the interpretation of borderline radiographic findings. A 90-year-old with small osteophytes presents a different clinical picture from a 45-year-old with identical radiographic appearances. Integrating structured clinical metadata alongside image features represents a necessary direction for future development, and is likely a prerequisite for automated grading systems to reach the sensitivity required for safe clinical deployment at the KL1 threshold.

### 4.4 Generalisability and Fairness

The absence of demographic metadata in the Kaggle dataset precludes any assessment of whether model performance varies across clinically important subgroups. Performance disparities across age, sex, race, and body habitus are a recognised concern in medical imaging AI [@obermeyer2019], and the arguments above suggest that age in particular may be a significant source of differential performance in KL grading — elderly patients may be systematically over-graded due to age-related normal osteophyte formation, while younger patients with genuine early OA may be under-graded. These disparities would be invisible in aggregate metrics such as QWK and overall accuracy, which is precisely why subgroup analysis is essential before clinical deployment.

The MRKR dataset [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr] was identified as a candidate for fairness evaluation given its demographic metadata across race, sex, age group, and weight-bearing status. However, domain shift arising from bilateral image acquisition precluded valid inference without retraining on matched data. This represents an important direction for future work — evaluating whether the KL1 detection improvements conferred by CORAL loss are consistent across demographic subgroups, or whether they disproportionately benefit certain populations while leaving others underserved.

### 4.5 Explainability

Grad-CAM analysis of shared misclassification cases revealed two predominant attention patterns. In over-grading cases — where true KL1 images were predicted as KL2 — models attended to minor osteophyte margins at the tibial plateau, suggesting the models were detecting genuine radiographic features but misinterpreting their severity. In under-grading cases — where true KL1 images were predicted as KL0 — activation was dispersed or peripherally displaced, indicating failure to attend to the subtle joint space narrowing that characterises early OA. This second pattern is the more clinically concerning, as it represents cases where the model found no relevant features at all rather than misinterpreting ones it detected.

Quantitative localisation scores (@fig-localisation) showed higher central activation for ResNet50+CE compared to ResNet50+CORAL, though this did not reflect superior clinical localisation on qualitative review — higher central scores in CE models reflected diffuse rather than targeted activation. This highlights a limitation of purely quantitative XAI metrics: a high localisation score does not guarantee that the model is attending to diagnostically relevant structures. Radiographer-led qualitative review remains an essential complement to quantitative explainability metrics, particularly for borderline grades where the relevant features are subtle and spatially precise [@teoh2023].

### 4.6 Model Trade-offs

Several practical trade-offs between configurations are worth noting for clinical deployment contexts. CORAL loss consistently improved QWK and KL1 recall but at a modest cost to overall accuracy and KL0 recall compared to CE. This trade-off is clinically acceptable — reducing missed early OA cases is more consequential than marginally lower normal classification accuracy, given the downstream implications of missing KL1 discussed above.

EfficientNet-B0 achieved marginally higher QWK and lower KL1 recall SD than ResNet50 across seeds, suggesting superior training stability, while requiring approximately six times fewer parameters (4 million versus 23.5 million) [@tan2019efficientnet; @he2016]. In a clinical deployment context where models may need to run on local hospital infrastructure rather than high-performance computing, this parameter efficiency is a practically relevant advantage. However, the performance difference between architectures was modest compared to the difference conferred by loss function choice, suggesting that loss function selection is the more impactful design decision for this task.

Finally, the three-seed evaluation demonstrated that all findings are stable across initialisations, with QWK SD ranging from 0.002 to 0.008 across configurations. This stability supports confidence in the observed differences and suggests that the results are unlikely to be attributable to favourable random initialisation.

## 5. Ethical Considerations

Automated KL grading systems carry several ethical considerations that must be addressed before clinical deployment. First, and most critically, such systems should function as decision support tools rather than replacements for clinical judgement. The KL1 recall rates reported in this study — even under the best configuration — indicate that no current automated system is sufficiently sensitive to operate independently at the early OA threshold without clinician oversight.

Second, the absence of demographic metadata in the training dataset means that potential performance disparities across age, sex, race, and body habitus remain unquantified. Deploying a system with unknown subgroup performance risks codifying existing healthcare inequalities, disproportionately affecting populations already underserved by diagnostic services [@obermeyer2019]. Prospective fairness evaluation across demographic subgroups is an ethical prerequisite for deployment, not an optional extension.

Third, transparency and explainability are essential for clinical trust. Grad-CAM provides partial insight into model attention but does not constitute a complete explanation of model decisions. Clinicians relying on automated grading outputs should be informed of the model's known failure modes — particularly its tendency to under-grade KL1 — to support appropriate scepticism when interpreting borderline cases.

Finally, both datasets used in this study are publicly available under open licences and contain no personally identifiable information, satisfying data governance requirements for research use.

## 6. Conclusion

This study demonstrates that loss function choice meaningfully affects early osteoarthritis detection in automated Kellgren-Lawrence grading. CORAL ordinal loss consistently improved KL1 recall by 86–168% compared to categorical cross-entropy across both CNN architectures (ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0) and three random seeds, while EfficientNet-B0 with CORAL achieved the highest overall QWK of 0.809, exceeding the 0.81 clinical benchmark [@landis1977]. However, all configurations remained below the meta-analytic KL1 sensitivity benchmark, indicating that ordinal loss is necessary but insufficient to fully resolve early OA detection failure. The persistent gap is attributable not only to modelling choices but to fundamental dataset limitations — the absence of acquisition metadata, age-related label noise, and the intrinsic radiographic heterogeneity of KL1. Future work should prioritise integration of clinical metadata, demographic fairness evaluation, and validation on demographically diverse datasets such as MRKR [@price2024emorykneeradiographmrkr] to establish whether these findings generalise to real clinical populations.

## References
